observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management:
a. Adopt a uniform standard
policy in depreciating PPEs, in
compliance with the
Accounting Policies provided in
Section 4(o), Volume I of the
New Government Accounting
System (NGAS) Manual for
Local Government Units and
apply the same across all PPE
items to come up with the
correct Accumulated
Depreciation for the affected
accounts; and
b. Direct the Municipal
Accountant to consider the
following adjusting journal
entries to correct the
misstatement of the
Accumulated Depreciation for
the affected Property, Plant
and Equipment accounts (see
AAR for suggested entries).
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented (Reiterated in
Part II of this Report) Some PPEs
implemented were not provided
with Residual Values; the
recognized expired life of some
PPEs in the books of accounts
were erroneous; and estimated
useful lives of some PPEs are
unrealistic.
observations.reason:
No action taken. Verification of
the affected accounts is on-going
and suggested correcting entries
were not considered as the
verification of the affected
accounts is still being
undertaken.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management require the
Municipal Accountant to
regularly monitor the Accounts
Payable account in order to
ensure that all transactions
affecting the account are
correctly recorded in the
books of accounts.
We further recommended that
the Municipal Accountant draw
a Journal Entry Voucher to
correct the noted errors. The
suggested correcting entry is as
follows:
Accounts Payable 1,061,271.38
PYA 1,061,271.38
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented (Reiterated in
Part II of this Report) No review and
analysis of AP was done due to
lack of workforce as a result of
the resignation or transfer of
staffs for medical reasons. Further,
they focused on the longtime issue
on PPE. We further stated that a
JEV No. 2024-09-2456 was
prepared to correct noted errors
for this specific transaction.
observations.reason:
Lack of workforce as a result of
resignations or transfers; focus on
other longstanding issues (PPE).
Some corrective entries were
prepared but full review not yet
completed.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management:
a. Oblige the inventory
committee to regularly
conduct a physical count of all
PPEs and to reconcile the
results of the count with the
property and accounting
records;
b. Require the MAccO, the
MTO and the Inventory
Committee to reconcile their
records/reports to come up with
a reliable PPE account balance
in the financial statements;
c. Direct the MTO and the
MAccO to maintain updated
property / ledger cards for the
other PPEs to facilitate
reconciliation of their records
and immediate verification of
discrepancies; and
d. Conduct a one-time
cleansing of the PPE accounts
of the Municipality in strict
compliance with the guidelines
and procedures set under COA
Circular No. 2020-006 to come
up with reliable PPE balances
that are verifiable as to
existence, condition, and
accountability.
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented (Reiterated in
Part II of this Report) Physical count
was conducted partially. Updating
of property cards still in process.
Reconciliation of records is
currently being undertaken.
observations.reason:
Only partial physical count was
conducted. Reconciliation of
property and accounting records is
still on-going; workforce constraints
and slow updating of records.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management conduct a
preliminary engineering (feasibility)
study of all the proposed projects
in order to determine or establish
their viability as well as
conformance to land use and
zoning guidelines prescribed by
existing laws and to avoid
suspensions or delay of completion
of projects due to unresolved
issues or complaints.
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented (Closed) Feasibility
studies were conducted for proposed
infrastructure items above ₱10 Million
and implementation of infrastructure
projects.
observations.reason:
Some projects were still being
validated; implementation of
infrastructure projects was
ongoing and feasibility items
conducted for projects above
PHP10 Million.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
management coordinate with the
various National Agencies and
other Offices to secure the data
needed to establish a GAD
database to serve as basis in
identifying priority gender issues
that are crucial for the
Municipality’s planning,
programming, and policy
formulation pursuant to PCW-
DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01.
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented Training in CBMS was
conducted and data gathering is
ongoing. No database established
as of to date. Partial GAD database
was established as of CY 2024.
observations.reason:
No database established as of the
audit; data gathering and training
are ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management:
a. Instruct the Accountant to
maintain Ledger Cards and to
compute the inventory costs
using the Moving Average Cost,
or the unit cost from its latest
purchase, if moving average cost
per unit cannot be determined;
b. Direct the Accountant and the
GSO to reconcile their records
regularly to establish correct
inventory account balances, if
any; and,
c. Create an Inventory
Committee to regularly conduct
physical count of supplies and
materials and submit a copy of
the inventory report to the
accounting office, copy
furnished the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction:
Not Implemented (Reiterated in
Part II of this Report) Schedule of
Ledger of Inventory was prepared
but the workforce was lacking. A
reconciliation of records is on-going.
Inventory Committee was created
and physical count of supplies and
materials was conducted as of
December 31, 2024.
observations.reason:
Lack of workforce delayed complete
implementation; reconciliation and
updating of ledgers ongoing; physical
count eventually conducted.

Management:

evaluation of the money

year’s audit recommendation that

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management intensify the tax
collection campaign program to
enhance the collection of
RPT/SET receivables by availing
of the collection remedies by
administrative action or judicial
action pursuant to Section 256 of
RA No. 7160. The administrative
remedies maybe any of the
following:
➢ Seizure of personal property of
the taxpayer and sale thereof at
public auction;
➢ File civil suit with the proper
court; and
➢ Levy on real property and sale of
delinquent property of a useable
portion thereof at public auction.
observations.managementAction:
Fully Implemented (Closed) The RPT
is under provincial imposition, thus,
the list of delinquent taxpayers was
endorsed to the Provincial Gov’t. of
Benguet, which includes enforcement
of administrative remedies.
observations.reason:
However, no action was taken by
the Province as of the reporting date.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that
Management close, thru a SB
Resolution, all accounts under
special trust fund for which the
purpose of its creation cease to
exist due to completion of
programs related thereto, and
transfer the existing balance of
the said accounts to the general
fund to prevent incurrence of
unnecessary bank charges and
minimize number of accounts
being maintained that require
preparation of BRS.
observations.managementAction:
Fully Implemented Closure of 2
special accounts: There are two
accounts with non-moving
balances since the related
programs were completed. These
accounts were requested for
closing last February 2025. Two
accounts were completed.
observations.reason:
There were three LBP accounts
with non-moving balances; these
were completed and requests for
closing were submitted.

observations.recommendation:
1.8. We recommended and Management agreed that the Local Chief
Executive (LCE) require:

1.8.1. The Municipal Engineering Office to determine the costs of
each component of the road network assets and furnish the
Municipal Accounting Office and the Treasury Office/General
Services Office a copy of the segregated costs and complete
description of the LRN projects;

1.8.2. The Inventory Committee to conduct a physical count of the
LRN assets and reconcile the results with the records of the
Accounting Office and the Treasury Office; and

1.8.3. The Municipal Accounting Office to:
1.8.3.1. Compute the depreciation and accumulated
depreciation of the road network assets based on
the segregated costs from the Engineering Office
and draw a JEV to correct the noted errors; and

1.8.3.2. Draw a JEV to correct the noted errors on the Land
Improvements, Other Infrastructure Assets and
Other Structure accounts.

1.8.4. We further recommended and Management agreed that the
LCE enjoin the concerned office heads to strictly comply with
the accounting and reporting guidelines on the Local Road
Asset Management System prescribed by COA Circular No.
2015-008 in order to come up with a reliable local road
network account in the financial statements.

2.8. We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accounting Office to present investment in time deposits with short term
maturity as cash and cash equivalents for the fair presentation
of the financial position and cash flows of the municipality.

4.7. We recommended and Management agreed to:

compliance with the Accounting Policies provided in Section

same across all PPE items to come up with the correct
Accumulated Depreciation for the affected accounts; and

4.7.2. Direct the Municipal Accountant to consider the following
adjusting journal entries to correct the misstatement of the
Accumulated Depreciation for the affected Property, Plant
and Equipment accounts:

6.9. We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to regularly monitor the Accounts Payable account in
order to ensure that all transactions affecting the account are
correctly recorded in the books of accounts.

6.10. We further recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal
Accountant draw a Journal Entry Voucher to correct the noted errors.
The suggested correcting entry is as follows:
Accounts Payable 1,061,271.38
Prior Year Adjustment (Government Equity) 1,061,271.38

7.9. We recommended and Management agreed to:
7.9.1. Oblige the inventory committee to regularly conduct a
physical count of all PPEs and to reconcile the results of the
count with the property and accounting records;
7.9.2. Require the MAccO, the MTO and the Inventory Committee to
reconcile their records/reports to come up with a reliable PPE
account balance in the financial statements;
7.9.3. Direct the MTO and the MAccO to maintain updated
property/ledger cards for the other PPEs to facilitate
reconciliation of their records and immediate verification of
discrepancies; and
7.9.4. Conduct a one-time cleansing of the PPE accounts of the
Municipality in strict compliance with the guidelines and
procedures set under COA Circular 2020-006 to come up
with reliable PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence,
condition, and accountability.

8.9. We recommended and Management agreed to:
8.9.1. Require the Accounting Office to recognize in the books of
accounts the appropriate Allowance for Impairment for
possible losses; and
8.9.2. Request for write-off if the loans are proven to be
uncollectible by strictly following the guidelines and
procedures on the write-off of dormant receivables accounts
under COA Circular No. 2023-008 dated August 17, 2023.

9.11. In view of the foregoing, we recommended and management agreed
to:
9.11.1. Clarify with the donor the condition set in the Deed of
Donation and inform him of the marking requirement under
COA Circular No.75-6 and the prohibitions imposed by DILG
Memorandum Circular No. 2010-101. If the donor consents to
the removal of the prohibited markings, request that the Deed
of Donation be revised to such effect. Else, decline the
donation in view of the requirements and prohibitions
provided by the COA and DILG Circular/Memorandum
Circular; and
9.11.2. Fast track processing of registration of the vehicle should the
donor consent to the alterations required to comply with the
prescribed markings of government property and take up
accordingly in the accounting records.

10.7. We recommended and Management agreed to:
10.7.1. Require the mandatory evaluation of the reasonableness of
proposed or estimated cost of repair of motor vehicles to
ensure that the expenditures for repairs are within the
maximum limit set in COA Circular 2012-003; and
10.7.2. Appropriate funds for the purchase of brand new motor
vehicles as replacement of the old or frequently repaired
motor vehicles.

11.6. We recommended and Management agreed that the General Service
Office maintain a repair and maintenance record/history of each
equipment / vehicle owned and / or operated by the Municipality to aid
in the conduct of evaluation of the reasonableness of proposed
repairs of equipment and motor vehicles.

11.7. We also recommended and Management agreed to properly set-up the
electronic New Government Accounting System (e-NGAS) to facilitate
generation of the repairs and maintenance records of PPE.

11.8. We further recommended and Management further agreed that the
repair and maintenance history/record be attached to disbursement
vouchers for repairs and maintenance expenses as supporting
documents to facilitate the review or audit of the transactions.

11.9. Management commented that the computer where the e-NGAS was
installed encountered problem in linking the system. Nevertheless, the
issue was already referred to COA – Regional Office.

12.9. We recommended and Management agreed to:
12.9.1. Conduct proper validation of identified or proposed projects
to ensure that only feasible PPAs, free from any issues of
ownership of proposed locations and right-of-way, are
included in the AIP; and
12.9.2. Hire/employ casual employees or job order workers to assist
in the preparation of programs of work and supervision of
projects pursuant to Section 77 of the Local Government
Code of 1991 or consider sourcing out manpower services to
assist in the preparation of programs of works as well as in
project implementation.

13.10. We recommended and Management agreed to:
13.10.1. Review and update annually or at the beginning of each year
its LDRRMFIP to include the PPAs to be funded from the
unexpended DRRM Trust funds (QRF and DRRMF-MOOE)
from previous years, and
13.10.2. Implement the DRRMF PPAs as planned in order to lessen the
socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters and
disaster risks, including projected climate risks, and to
enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of
the Municipality.

14.5. We recommended and Management agreed to immediately remit to
the account of the Secretary of DPWH the latter’s share from
building permits fees in the amount of ₱65,555.12 and to strictly
enforce thereafter the full remittance of the 5% DPWH Share within
the prescribed period.

15.8. We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that all changes
in the GAD Plan and Budget are submitted to the DILG for their
appropriate disposition pursuant to Section 6 of JMC No. 2016-01.

15.9. We likewise recommended and Management agreed to thoroughly
review its GPB to avoid duplication of GAD activities.

We recommended that Management:
a. Require the Municipal Treasurer to regularly furnish the Accountant at the beginning of the year of a duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year; and
b. Require the Municipal Accountant to set-up Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables at the beginning of the year base on the duly certified list from the MTO showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year and record in the books of accounts the due and demandable Real Property Tax and Special Education Tax Receivables. observations.recommendation:
We recommend that management record the unutilized balance of DRRMF from Special Trust Fund after five years to the Unappropriated Surplus account of the general fund upon reversion. The suggested adjusting entry is as follows:
Particulars Debit Credit
Trust Liability (Social Services) 4,590,955.45
Prior Period Adjustment 4,590,955.45
To record unutilized DRRMF for CYs 2012-2016

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated the previous year’s audit recommendation that Management:
a. Reconcile the balances of the account in the books with the accounts of the source agencies (SA) and make the necessary adjustments;
b. Submit to the SAs the liquidation reports of the outstanding fund transfers.
c. Verify the implementation of projects funded out of transferred funds and return to the Source Agency any unused balance upon completion of the project.
d. Strictly abide with the rules and regulations in the grant, utilization and liquidation of fund transfer under COA- Circular 94-013.
observations.managementAction: No action taken.
observations.reason: Confirmation letters were issued to source agencies. The fund transfers from National Child Development Center amounting to ₱157,527.12 were settled. Reconciliation is ongoing.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management intensify the tax collection campaign program to enhance the collection of RPT/SET receivables through availing of the collection remedies by administrative action or judicial action pursuant to Section 256 of RA No. 7160. The administrative remedies maybe any of the following:
 Seizure of personal property of the taxpayer and sale thereof at public auction;
 File civil suit with the proper court; and
 Levy on real property and sale of delinquent property of a useable portion thereof at public auction.
We further recommend that the Municipal Assessor monitor the general revision of the assessment of properties every three years as required under Section 219 of RA 7160, to generate additional income from increased tax assessment.
observations.managementAction: No action taken. The certified list of all real property tax delinquencies was submitted to the Sanggunian (Both Municipal and Provincial), since this is a provincial imposition.
observations.reason: The certified list of real property delinquencies was submitted to the Sanggunian. The RPT general revision of assessment is implemented in CY 2023.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
a. Review and update annually or at the beginning of each year its LDRRMFIP to include the PPAs to be funded from the unexpended DRRM funds (QRF and DRRMF-MOOE) from previous years to facilitate the timely implementation of the DRRM PPAs; 
b. Implement the DRRMF PPAs as planned in order to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters and disaster risks, including projected climate risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the Municipality; and
c. Account and monitor properly the utilization of the DRRMF in order to reflect in the Notes to the Financial Statements its correct balances.
observations.managementAction: Supplemental AIP is prepared which included the PPAs to be funded from the unexpended DRRM Funds.
observations.reason: Utilization in CY 2023 was 51% compared to 38.40% in CY 2022. Utilization was reflected accordingly in the Notes to FS.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:

b. Require the Accounting Office to recognize in the books of accounts the appropriate Allowance for Impairment for the possible losses; and
c. Request for write-off if the loans are proven to be uncollectible by strictly following the guidelines and procedures on the write-off of dormant receivables accounts under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

observations.reason: Locations/address of the other borrowers are currently being validated. Documents needed for write-off are being prepared; needs ample time to prove/verify the non-collectability of said loan accounts.

observations.recommendation:

b. Hire/employ casual employees or job order workers to assist in the preparation of programs of work and supervision of projects pursuant to Section 77 of the Local Government Code of 1991; and

observations.managementAction: Validations were conducted; however, some issues were not resolved. Hiring for job orders was posted but no applicants for the said positions. Supplemental Budgets for CY 2023 were prepared in the last quarter.

observations.recommendation:
1.10 We reiterated our previous audit recommendations that Management:
a. Create an inventory committee to conduct a physical count of PPEs
and submit the RPCPPE to the MAccO, copy furnished the Audit
Team;
b. Require the MAccO to ensure that property records are reconciled
with the accounting records to come up with a reliable PPE account
balance in the financial statements; and
c. Ensure that property cards and ledger cards must at all times be
maintained by the MTO and MAccO, respectively, to facilitate
reconciliation of the property and accounting records and immediate
verification of discrepancies.

2.8 We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make the
appropriate correcting journal entry to recognize the Road Networks
account in the books of accounts in accordance to DILG MC No. 2018-61
and COA Circular 2015-008 to reflect the correct balance of the Asset
account of the municipality. The suggested correcting entry is as
follows:
Account Debit Credit
Road Networks ₱3,161,109.15
Government Equity ₱3,161,109.15

3.9 We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to enjoin the
concerned office heads’ strict compliance with the accounting and
reporting guidelines on the Local Road Asset Management System
prescribed by COA Circular No. 2015-008.

4.7 We recommended and management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant to maintain the required Registries of Public Infrastructures
to ensure proper identification and recording of these assets into the
books of accounts until all the infrastructure projects of the municipality
are fully recognized.

8.4 We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to revert the
unutilized balance of DRRMF amounting to P2.542 million to the
unappropriated surplus of the General Fund to be made available for other
social services after subsequent enactment by the local sanggunian as
prescribed for in Section 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002.

10.7 We reiterated the prior years’ audit recommendation that Management
submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on a monthly basis
to the Audit Team in compliance with COA Circular No. 2012-002.

11.7 We reiterated the previous years’ audit recommendation that Management
implement the DRRMF PPAs as planned in order to lessen the socio-economic
and environmental impacts of disasters and disaster risks, including projected
climate risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of
the Municipality.
11.8 The Municipal Engineer claimed that most of the unutilized amount of the
LDRRMF were capital outlay. Furthermore, there was a delay in the procurement
process on the use of the Quick Response Fund since the declaration was made only
sometime December, 2018.

12.8 We recommended that the Municipality:
1. Prepare a comprehensive ten-year SWM Program which is consistent with
the 19 National Solid Waste Management Framework in compliance with
RA No. 9003 so that it can be finally submit to the National Solid Waste
Management Commission (NSWMC) for review and approval; and
2. Fasten the provision of collection services to residents to prevent
indiscriminate disposal of wastes which may pose hazards to health and
safety of the public and the environment.
12.9 The Municipal Engineering commented that the technical part of the SWM Plan was
completed. The SWM Program according to the Municipal Panning and
Development Officer was submitted to the Benguet Provincial Government for
review.

13.5 We recommended and the Municipality agreed to establish a GAD M&E system
as required under Sections 3.4 and 5.1 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No.
2013-01.

14.4 We recommended and management agreed to require members of the GFPS
to:
a. Institutionalize the GAD database with adequate and systematically
gathered sex-disaggregated data which will provide the bases for gender
analysis, planning, programming, and policy formulation;
b. Undertake gender analysis to identify and confirm existing gender issues
and ensure that the PPAs are responsive to the said issues; and
c. Revisit Annex B of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01 which
identifies the data requirements for particular gender issues.

15.8 We reiterated prior years’ recommendation that the municipality, during the
preparation of the GAD Accomplishment Report, administer the HGDG test on
the GAD PPAs as basis in determining the actual expenditure that can be
attributed to the GAD budget pursuant to Item 4.1.C.4.5.e of JMC No. 2013-01.

16.5 We recommended that henceforth, the MSB ensure that ECCD PPAs are
considered in the preparation of the SEF annual budget pursuant to Section 7(b)
of RA No. 10410 and JC No. 1.
16.6 The representative from the Municipal School Board explained that they did not
include in the SEF budget the ECCD programs because these were already included
in the PPAs of the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Office. Nevertheless,
management will refer the matter with the MSB for possible inclusion in the 2020
budget.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended management to implement the programs, projects and activities (PPAs) in the year these were programmed to avoid accumulation of continuing projects and allow timely delivery of services to beneficiaries.
observations.managementAction: Management implemented prior year’s projects, thus implementation of current year’s projects were 36 percent unimplemented.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management
Ensure that all collection transactions are recorded in the books within the accounting period of collection.

Require the Municipal Treasurer to monitor all field collectors to turn over their collections at least once a week or as soon as their collections reach P5,000.00
observations.managementAction:
Municipal Treasury office recorded collections within the required period.

The Municipal Treasurer received all collections from field collectors.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Sangguniang Bayan extend assistance in the improvement of tax collection through the issuance of appropriate legislations.
observations.managementAction: Management referred the matter to the Sangguniang Bayan (SB) with the list of delinquent taxes.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that management secure from the Associations or from the National Irrigation Administration project documents relative to the implementation of the irrigation projects and any contract documents relative to the releases of project funds to identified beneficiaries.

We have recommended that management enforce payments of the loans in accordance with the amortization schedule.
observations.managementAction:
Management requested from the NIA the necessary documents. However, no document was furnished to the municipality.



Demand letters were sent to concerned debtors/associations annually.
observations.reason:
Partially Implemented.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the SB Municipality amend its Revenue Code and reduce its tax rates in accordance with the maximum rates prescribed under Section 143 of Republic Act No. 7160.
observations.managementAction: Municipality referred the matter to Committee on Ways and Means For monitoring of compliance by management requested more time to study the matter.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Officials implement their Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Plan as mandated by law in order to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of the disasters in the community and disaster risks, including projected climate risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness of the barangay and response capabilities of the community.
observations.managementAction: Management implemented their Local downloaded by A total of P12,791,602.45 (WFP), thus the unexpended and MDRMMF of Continuing Appropriation of P9,522,616.46 and Current Year P 3,268,985.99.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management require the Department of Education to replenish the ineligible disbursements from the SEF and the Municipality to replenish from its regular fund, the cost of the vehicle and the disbursements for gasoline, repair and maintenance.
observations.managementAction: No actions taken to require the Department of Education to replenish the ineligible SEF disbursements.
observations.reason: For monitoring of compliance by management

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant record the issuances of the inventories to the requisitioning offices as they take place to ensure that issuances of balances of inventory accounts are accurate and reliable. The moving average method shall be used to determine the cost of inventory.
We have further recommended that the municipality conduct the required physical count of inventories at least twice a year to verify their actual existence and condition.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accounting Office claimed that it had no basis in recording Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI) and other documents submitted by the Treasury Office. The availability of inventories held for consumption had yet to be determined by concerned committee.
observations.reason: For monitoring of compliance by management since there was no report of Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued

observations.recommendation: We have recommended and management agreed that a. the Supply Officer nor designate prepare and submit any SSMIs to the Accounting Office for it to effect the necessary adjustment to the inventory accounts; and b) the Treasury Office, RHU and Dental Clinic reconcile the inventory balances on hand with the Accountant’s records to reflect accurate inventory accounts in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: The designated Supply Officer nor submitted any SSMIs to the Accounting Office. Reiterated in Part II of 2014 AAR. No reconciliation was made between the Treasury Office, RHU and Dental Clinic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and management agreed to refrain from disbursing reimbursement of communication expenses to its officials and employees through cash advance in accordance with the provisions of Section 3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.

We recommended that management:
a. Submit supporting expense receipts for claims of reimbursement for communication expenses for CY 2016; and
b. Consider availing of mobile communication post-paid plans to have a documentary trail of communication costs incurred by each official entitled to reimbursement of communication expense.

We recommended management the following:
a. Limit the utilization of the SEF to the allowed expenses as prescribed by the rules and regulations, and
b. Require DepEd schools concerned to replenish the ineligible expenses in the amount of P489,016.50 disbursed from the SEF.

We recommended that the ineligible expenses charged to MDF amounting to P103,022.66 be reimbursed from the general fund. We further recommended that the 20 percent MDF be strictly utilized in accordance with the general guidelines under DILG-DBM Joint Circular No. 2011-1.

We recommended and Management agreed to consider the following:
a. Ensure that only feasible Programs/Projects/Activities, free from any issues of ownership and right-of-way, are included in the Annual Investment Plan; and
b. Formulate/develop specific strategies/solutions to effectively implement development projects on a timely basis. Consider formulating a timetable within which to start and complete the unimplemented projects.

We recommended and management agreed to:
a. Refrain from granting cash advances to an elective officials other than for official travelling expenses in consonance COA Circular No. 97-002; and
b. Cash advances for MOOE should only be only granted to permanently appointed Disbursing Officer.

We recommended that the municipality:
a. Fast track the preparation of the required ten-year SWMP in compliance with RA No. 9003.
b. Consider providing the residents’ alternative means of waste containment while there is no collection of garbage being done by the municipality to prevent indiscriminate disposal which may pose hazards to health and safety of the public and the environment.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
a. Conduct a needs assessment to determine trainings and similar activities that will contribute to the improvement of their capability development;
b. Formulate a CapDevAgenda, which will serve as a guide in the implementation of trainings; and
c. Ensure that no study tours, training, programs and other similar activities will be implemented without the approved CapDev Agenda of the municipality and the proper endorsement of the DILG.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
a. The municipality conduct a needs assessment to determine trainings and seminar to be attended and the participants to each training or seminar shall be limited to those whose functions are relevant to the said training/seminar; and
b. Management to delimit their attendance to trainings, seminars, workshops or similar activities funded out of public funds, especially those held in places outside of their official stations or territorial jurisdictions. Personnel of the Municipality are urged to take full cognizance of their principal responsibility and accountability relative to the prudent use of their scarce financial resources.

We recommended and management agreed to:
a. Administer the HGDG test on its GAD PPAs, the results of which shall be the basis in estimating the cost of gender mainstreaming and the Gender and Development Budget; and
b. Administer the HGDG test on its GAD PPAs during the preparation of the GAD AR as basis in determining the actual expenditure that can be attributed to the GAD Budget pursuant to JMC No. 2013-01.

We recommended and management agreed to require the BAC, in subsequent procurement transactions, properly and completely observe the standards and regulations of procurement activities as provided in RA No. 9184 and its IRR.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to maintain a separate books of accounts for the PCF in compliance with PhilHealth Circular No. 40 and for the monitoring of the receipts, proper allocations, dispositions and balances of the fund.

We recommended and the municipal accountant agreed to prepare adjusting entries to reclassify the Other Payables account to Trust Liabilities- DRRMF for the proper presentation of liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position.
observations.managementAction: The accounting office made the adjusting entries through JEV no. 2016-01-00004.
observations.reason: With the implementation of PPSAS, the fund was classified to its proper account code.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the accounting office agreed to make the appropriate adjustments on the affected accounts in accordance with the procedures prescribed under Section 107 of the NGAS Manual, Volume I for Local Government Units, and COA Circular No. 2015-009.
observations.managementAction: The accounting office made the appropriate adjustments through JEV No. 2016-06-000023.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant make the necessary adjustments to correct the Cash-Local Treasury account balance. Likewise, require the Municipal Treasurer to regularly tally her collections and deposits to avoid the incurrence of any over and/or under deposit.
observations.managementAction: The over-deposit had been counterbalanced from succeeding deposits made by the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: The Cash-Local Treasury reflected a debit balance at year-end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management:
 Require the accountable officers to immediately liquidate/ refund their cash advances to avoid the withholding of their salaries or the institution of other sanctions;  Instruct the Municipal accountant to closely monitor the liquidation of cash advances granted to accountable officers within the prescribed periods; otherwise, withhold their salaries until the settlement of their accounts; and  Strictly adhere to the prohibition that no cash advances shall be granted until those previously granted outstanding advances are settled or liquidated to avoid the accumulation of unliquidated cash advances.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant required the immediate liquidation/refund of cash advances. As of December 31, 2016 only P300.00 remained unliquidated. The Municipal Accountant monitored the liquidation of cash advances granted to accountable officers. No additional cash advances were granted for those who have outstanding cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Treasury Office devise procedures to facilitate the preparation of the abstract of collection so that the share of the barangays from real property tax collections can be released within five days after the end of each quarter pursuant to Section 271 (d) of the Local Government Code to afford the barangays timely implementation of their programs and projects.
observations.managementAction: RPT shares of barangays for 1st and 2nd quarters of CY 2016 had been released as of July, 2016.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the personnel officer require those concerned to submit the required supporting documents to justify the monetization of 50 percent of their leave credits; otherwise, this shall be disallowed for being an irregular disbursement of funds.
We further recommended that the agency strictly observe the existing CSC rules and strictly observe regulations on the conditions for the monetization of 50 percent of leave credits of the officials and employees.
observations.managementAction: The personnel officer required the submission of supporting documents and copy furnished the Audit Team. Supporting documents were submitted to justify the monetization of 50 percent of the leave credits of the employees concerned. Management committed to strictly observe CSC rules and regulations for monetization of 50 percent of leave credits.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the officials and employees who availed themselves of the optical, dental and examinations refund the costs pertinent thereto and may claim reimbursement, as applicable, from the PhilHealth under the National Health Insurance Act. The Accountant shall compute the amount to be refunded by each official and employee concerned. We further recommended that the municipality deduct from payrolls the disbursements for medical, dental and optical examinations not covered under Administrative Order No. 402.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Mayor issued a directive dated August 15, 2016 to all municipal officials and employees. As of December 31, 2016 a total of P59,289.92 had been deducted from the payrolls. The balance of which was fully paid as of March 2017. Payroll deduction started in October 2016 up to present.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the agency refrain from splitting contracts into smaller purchase quantities and amounts when requests of these are of the same nature and purpose. All procurement shall be done through competitive bidding. Alternative modes of procurement shall be resorted to only under the conditions stated in Republic Act No. 9184.
We further recommended that the entity abide by the mode of procurement indicated in the APP of the agency. Any change in the mode of procurement shall be justified by the BAC and approved by the agency head.
observations.managementAction: Management abided with the mode of procurement as indicated in the APP of the agency.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the above enumerated supporting documents to enable us to make a final disposition of the disbursements.
observations.managementAction: A certification from the Mt. Sinai Diagnostic Clinic was submitted with the names of the Municipal officials and employees and corresponding laboratory examinations they availed. The supporting documents were submitted with the response of the Municipal Health Officer considered.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the officials and employees responsible for the implementation of the contract for the construction of the building and the continuous payment of the progress billings, submit justification on why they should not be cited for gross neglect of duty and be subjected to disciplinary actions as provided under Section 60 of Republic Act 7160, for failure to terminate the contract and take over the project or award the unfinished portion to a qualified contractor as provided under R.A. No. 9184 despite the gross inefficiencies committed by the contractor in the prosecution of the project.

We recommended that the municipality, thru the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer, require the contractors to post warranty security from the defects liability period or final acceptance in the form and during the warranty period as prescribed under Section 62 of the RIRR.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a monitoring report on this matter on a monthly basis, copy furnished the Audit Team for its perusal.
observations.managementAction: The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Engineer required the posting of warranty security for projects where required.
observations.reason: required.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended the following:
1. The municipality to immediately terminate the contract and undertake by administration the unfinished work, or award the same to another qualified contractor that the municipality will be able to collect the liquidated damages from the contractor. The termination of the contract shall be accompanied by the confiscation of the contractor’s performance security and a recommendation for blacklisting of the contractor.
2. The municipal accountant to compute and collect from the contractor liquidated damages for the negative slippage incurred. Submit also justification why the liquidated damages was not computed and deducted from the progress billings of the contractor.
observations.managementAction:
The municipality allowed the contractor to continue with the project so the municipality will be able to collect the liquidated damages from the contractor.
The municipal accountant collected from the contractor the amount of P1,313,071.58 representing the maximum ten percent of the contract price as liquidated damages.
observations.reason: It was already enforce the recommendation because the project was already completed and the liquidated damages collected from the contractor.

observations.managementAction: Management used the petty cash fund to pay expenses not exceeding P1,000.00. PCF was not closed at the end of the year. The amount of P8,873.49 remained in the petty cash fund as of year-end. PCF was reduced from P100,000.00 to P50,000.00. The Municipal Accountant recorded the amount of P94,080.88.
